b1plus-tag-questions.title: Seeking Confirmation (ғой, ...емес пе?)
b1plus-tag-questions.explanation: To seek agreement or confirmation — like English tag questions ("..., isn't it?") — Kazakh uses two common devices. The particle ғой softens a statement into something like "..., you know / right?": Ол келеді ғой (He's coming, right?). Attaching ...емес пе? ("isn't it/aren't you") to the end of a clause makes an explicit tag question: Сен шаршадың, емес пе? (You're tired, aren't you?)
b1plus-tag-questions.fact: Ғой is one of the most frequent particles in casual Kazakh speech, because it does social work beyond just asking: it signals shared knowledge between speaker and listener, softening a statement so it doesn't sound like a flat assertion.

b1plus-event-nominalization.title: Turning Verbs into Event Nouns (-у, -уы)
b1plus-event-nominalization.explanation: Adding -у to a verb stem creates a verbal noun naming the action itself: келу ("coming"), бару ("going"). Adding a possessive suffix on top — келуі ("his/her coming") — turns it into a noun phrase that can be the subject or object of another clause, letting Kazakh embed whole events inside a sentence without a separate conjunction like English "that": Оның келуі мені қуантты (His coming made me happy).
b1plus-event-nominalization.fact: This -у/-уы pattern is the workhorse behind constructions already familiar from simpler grammar points — "маған баруым керек" (I need to go) is built exactly this way, with баруым ("my going") functioning as the grammatical subject of керек.

b1plus-intensive-reduplication.title: Intensifying Reduplication (жап-жақсы, қап-қара)
b1plus-intensive-reduplication.explanation: Kazakh intensifies certain adjectives — mostly colors and a handful of common qualities — by reduplicating the first syllable's onset sound plus a linking consonant, then attaching the full adjective: қара (black) → қап-қара (jet black); жақсы (good) → жап-жақсы (really good); жасыл (green) → жап-жасыл (bright green). This is a fixed, closed set of intensifiers, not a productive rule for any adjective.
b1plus-intensive-reduplication.fact: Unlike English "very," which combines freely with any adjective, Kazakh's intensifying reduplication only works for a specific, memorized list of adjectives — the linking consonant is essentially fixed per word rather than predictable from a general rule.

items.en: Tomorrow is a day off, isn't it?
The children's playing can be heard.
We need to preserve our mother tongue.
The finishing of the work took a long time.
